title: QOuLiPo: What a quantum computer sees when it reads a book
abstract: What does a book look like to a quantum computer? This paper takes eight classical works of the Renaissance and its late-antique inheritance -- from Augustine to Galileo -- and runs each through a neutral-atom quantum processor. The bridge is graphs: each textual unit becomes an atom, and graph edges are physical blockade constraints for engineered exact unit-disk designs, or a 2D approximation to the semantic graph for natural texts.   Three contributions follow. First, we introduce rigidity rho, a metric for how unique a book's structural backbone is -- distinguishing Marguerite de Navarre's Heptameron (rigid, twelve-nouvelle hard core) from Boethius (fully fungible, every chapter substitutable). Second, we invert the pipeline: rather than extracting a graph from existing prose, we pick a target graph the hardware encodes natively, and write a book whose structure matches it. The twenty-nine texts written this way, collected under the name QOuLiPo, extend the OuLiPo tradition to graph-topological constraints and, together with the eight natural texts, form a benchmark distribution against which neutral-atom hardware can be tracked as it scales. Third, we run both natural and engineered texts on Pasqal's FRESNEL processor up to one hundred atoms; engineered texts reach high approximation ratios, the cleanest instances returning the exact backbone.   A cloud-accessible quantum machine plus an agentic coding environment now lets a single investigator run this pipeline end-to-end. What is reported is an application layer, not a speedup -- humanistic instances ready to load onto neutral-atom processors as they scale, already complementing classical text analysis. The Digital Humanities community has a stake in building familiarity with this hardware now: the engineered-corpus design choices made today fix the benchmark distribution future hardware will be measured against.
summary: This paper presents a framework for mapping the semantic structure of books onto neutral-atom quantum processors using the Maximum Independent Set problem. By creating an engineered corpus called QOuLiPo, the authors provide a new benchmark for tracking the scaling of Rydberg-based quantum hardware. The work bridges quantum computing and digital humanities through a new metric called 'rigidity'.
main_problem: Exploring how a quantum computer perceives textual structure by mapping semantic graphs of books to physical atom registers to solve the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem.
main_result: The authors successfully used a neutral-atom processor to recover the structural backbone of both natural and engineered texts, introducing a 'rigidity' metric to quantify textual uniqueness.
method: An end-to-end pipeline uses LLM-based OCR and embeddings to transform text into k-nearest neighbor graphs, which are then mapped to Rydberg atom arrays using pulse and register engineering.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a neutral-atom quantum processor (Pasqal FRESNEL) utilizing the Rydberg blockade effect to implement the constraints of the MIS problem via the Rydberg Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Rigidity (rho), Backbone size (MIS/N), Approximation ratio, and Edge recall.
important_parameters: Number of atoms (N), blockade radius (Rb), k-nearest neighbor parameter (k), and cosine similarity threshold (t).
main_assumptions: The primary information loss occurs during the 2D embedding of the semantic graph, and the structural conclusions are sensitive to the choice of embedding model.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Rydberg blockade mechanism and mapping text to atoms; Figure 2 illustrates the end-to-end pipeline; Figure 3 plots the corpus rigidity; Figure 4 compares engineered vs. natural graphs; Figure 10 shows the impact of atom placement on the approximation ratio.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of reading books via quantum hardware, describes the pipeline for graph construction and atom register design, presents the QOuLiPo engineered corpus, evaluates hardware performance on natural and engineered texts, and discusses the implications for the Digital Humanities.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a novel application of Rydberg blockade-based computing to non-physical, semantic graphs, testing the limits of hardware-native optimization and register engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Neutral-atom MIS in one picture. (a) Rydberg blockade. Two atoms held within a blockade radius Rb cannot both be driven to the excited Rydberg state |r⟩: the doubly-excited level |rr⟩is shifted up by an interaction energy U = C6/r6 ij much larger than the Rabi coupling Ωthat the laser would need to populate it, so only the symmetric singly-excited combination (|gr⟩+ |rg⟩)/ √

Figure 2: End-to-end pipeline on Giambullari’s 1544 treatise: (1) original page photograph, (2) fine-tuned vision-language-model OCR (VLM-OCR) at 2.48% character error rate (CER) [33], (3) k-NN text graph, (4) 2D QPU and 3D emulator registers, (5) bitstring histograms.

Figure 3: The corpus laid out along the rigidity axis ρ ∈[0, 1], between the fully fungible limit (ρ = 0, every selected unit has a structural substitute) and the unique backbone limit (ρ = 1, every selected unit is irreplaceable). Blue circles: natural texts (at the knn each text is most discriminating at; see Table 1). Red diamonds: engineered QOuLiPo texts written for this paper.

Figure 4: Register targets, with the classical MIS highlighted on each graph. Top row — QOuLiPo (engineered) texts: graphs designed by construction as exact unit-disk or unit-ball graphs. Bottom row — natural texts: k-nearest-neighbour graphs in multilingual sentence-embedding space. Red nodes are members of an exact MIS computed by ILP.

Figure 5: The full QOuLiPo corpus (twenty-nine engineered texts) in the three-dimensional design space (design tier × geometric class × rigidity). Each marker is one engineered text; markers are slightly offset within each (tier, geometric-class, ρ) bucket so all 29 are visible. Red diamonds: corpus members run on the QPU (FRESNEL_CAN1). Blue circles: corpus members run only on the emulator. Marker size scales with N.
